transcript.query: treatment for Stage 3b CKD with considerations for specific lab abnormalities and medication interactions

literature.text: CLINICAL STATEMENTS AND GUIDELINES November 14, 2023  Circulation. 2023;148:1606–1635. DOI: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001184 Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory: Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Health 1624 CKD and ASCVD In patients with concomitant ASCVD and CKD, moder- ate- to high-intensity statins are recommended as part of optimal medical therapy for ASCVD and CKD.41 Medi- cations that preserve kidney function and reduce car- diovascular death such as SGTL2 inhibitors should be ­prioritized, regardless of diabetes status.121 For those who have not achieved individualized glycemic targets despite metformin and SGLT2 inhibitor or who are un- able to use those medications, a long-acting GLP-1RA is recommended.103 Finerenone can be considered to reduce adverse cardiovascular and kidney events in ­patients with diabetes and CKD already on maximally tolerated renin-angiotensin system inhibition,104 with or without SGLT2 inhibitor use. CKD and HF The systematic underuse of high-quality evidence-based therapies for CVD in patients with kidney disease, his- torically referred to as renalism,122 remains a significant barrier to GDMT optimization in HF with CKD. This para- digm extends to...

CLINICAL STATEMENTS AND GUIDELINES November 14, 2023  Circulation. 2023;148:1636–1664. DOI: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001186 Ndumele et al Science and Clinical Management of CKM Syndrome 1654 GLP-1RAs such as coexisting ASCVD, multiple comor- bidities, or high levels of excess weight/glycemia should be further investigated and clarified. Further data are also needed on whether GLP-1RA use is similarly safe and ef- fective in patients with HFpEF and HFrEF. Lipid-Lowering Therapies Beyond Statins in Diabetes and High CKM Risk Management of dyslipidemia is at the heart of ASCVD reduction for people with CVD and diabetes, who fre- quently fall into a very high-risk category because of high event rates. For individuals with diabetes and AS- CVD, high-intensity statins are recommended first-line lipid-lowering therapy. Based on clinical trial data, both American and European guidelines support the addi- tion of ezetimibe, followed by PCSK9 inhibitors when needed, as reasonable for very high-risk patients with diabetes and ASCVD on maximally tolerated statins who have not achieved a 50% reduction of LDL-C or with an LDL-C >70 mg/dL.62,63 American and European guide- lines differ on the use of...

CLINICAL STATEMENTS AND GUIDELINES November 14, 2023  Circulation. 2023;148:1606–1635. DOI: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001184 Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory: Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Health 1612 Stage 3: Subclinical CVD in CKM Syndrome Stage 3 is defined as subclinical CVD among individu- als with excess/dysfunctional adiposity, metabolic risk factors, or CKD. This includes imaging markers of sub- clinical atherosclerotic CVD (ASCVD; most frequently an increased coronary artery calcium [CAC] score on cardiac computed tomography but also potentially re- flected by nonobstructive coronary artery disease on coronary angiography or by subclinical peripheral ar- tery disease) or subclinical HF, ascertained through either elevation in cardiac biomarkers (eg, BNP [B- type natriuretic peptide], NT-proBNP [N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide], high-sensitivity car- diac troponins) or abnormal cardiovascular structure or function on myocardial imaging in the absence of clinical symptoms. The presence of subclinical CVD has been associated with an increased absolute risk of future CVD and, in some cases, an increased risk of mortality.51–54 Given the focus on high absolute CV...

greater than the MIC of the infecting organism. Many investigators recommend at least one or at least a weekly Cmin level obtained at either 12, 18, or 24 hours after the aminoglycoside dose.267–270 The Cmin level should be below the limits of detection of the assay (o1 mg/ml) at these time intervals. Measuring aminoglycoside levels with multiple-daily dosing strategies have been standardized for Cmax to be obtained 30 minutes after a 30-minute infusion, and Cmin right before the next dose for trough levels. The aminoglyco- sides should be administered in patients who are volume- replete; volume depletion increases the risk of nephrotoxicity in experimental studies and is suggested in clinical studies. Additionally, potassium repletion has been shown experi- mentally and clinically to diminish the risk of AKI related to aminoglycoside administration. Single-dose daily regimens are difﬁcult to apply in patients with pre-existing kidney disease, and patients with vacillating eGFR and hemodynamics, such as critically ill patients in the ICU setting. The changing pharmacokinetics and pharma- codynamics of antibiotics in general and aminoglycosides in particular, in the critically ill p...

CLINICAL STATEMENTS AND GUIDELINES November 14, 2023  Circulation. 2023;148:1606–1635. DOI: 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001184 Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory: Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Health 1622 Chronic Kidney Disease Addressing comorbid traditional CVD risk factors re- mains key in patients with CKD. For hypertension, especially in the setting of proteinuria, which is an in- dependent CVD risk factor (particularly for HF), ACE inhibitors/ARBs are the first-line treatment. The use of SGLT2 inhibitors should be extended to all patients with CKD (at moderate or higher risk per the KDIGO classification), regardless of diabetes status, to ­protect kidney function and to reduce rates of HF hospitaliza- tion and cardiovascular mortality.103 SGLT2 inhibitors can be safely initiated for patients with eGFR ≥20 mL∙min−1∙1.73 m−2. In patients with diabetic kidney disease and proteinuria on ACE inhibitors/ARBs, fi- nerenone may be considered to reduce adverse car- diovascular and kidney events.104 Finerenone can likely be initiated on background SGLT2 inhibitor therapy for those with eGFR >25 mL∙min−1∙1.73 m−2 and potas- sium <5 mEq/L, although definitive data on concurrent use of t...

patient_summary:
Patient context:
- Demographics: 88yo M; data window 2020 - 2022; (not retrieved)
- CKD stage: Stage 3b
- Prior admissions in t0: 1; types: EW EMER.
- Recent labs (latest values, with units and dates):
  - Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL (2117-04-30)
- Current/recent medications: 
  - Acetaminophen (2117-04-21 to 2117-05-01)
  - Atorvastatin (2117-04-21 to 2117-05-01)
  - Aspirin (2117-04-22 to 2117-05-01)
  - amLODIPine (2117-04-30 to 2117-05-01)
  - HydrALAZINE (2117-04-26 to 2117-05-01)
- Active comorbidities:
  - I129: Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease
  - Z85828: Personal history of other malignant neoplasm of skin
  - N1830: Chronic kidney disease, stage 3 unspecified
  - Z87891: Personal history of nicotine dependence
  - E8770: Fluid overload, unspecified
recommendation:
For this 88-year-old male patient with CKD Stage 3b, safe and effective cardiovascular and renal protection is crucial, especially given his personal history of hypertension and CKD. His recent creatinine level was 1.2 mg/dL, and he is experiencing fluid overload. Current medications include atorvastatin and multiple antihypertensives, which indicate management of cardiovascular risk factors.

1. **SGLT2 Inhibitor (e.g., empagliflozin):**
   - **Why this patient:** With CKD stage 3b and a creatinine level of 1.2 mg/dL, the patient could benefit from SGLT2 inhibitors, which are noted to preserve kidney function and reduce cardiovascular mortality irrespective of diabetes status [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19)]. This aligns with the need for renal protection and fluid management due to his fluid overload [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17)].
   - **When to use / contraindications:** SGLT2 inhibitors are appropriate in patients with an eGFR of at least 20 mL/min/1.73 m². They should be used cautiously if there is severe impairment in kidney function beyond this threshold or if the patient experiences volume depletion [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17); Circulation. 2023 (ndumele_ckm_synopsis_2023_p19)].
   - **Supporting sources:** [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17)], [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19)].

2. **Statin (Atorvastatin ongoing or adjustment):**
   - **Why this patient:** The patient is already on atorvastatin, which is recommended as high-intensity statin therapy to manage dyslipidemia and cardiovascular risk in those with CKD and hypertension [Ndumele et al Science and Clinical Management (ndumele_ckm_synopsis_2023_p19)]. This is crucial given the potential for concurrent ASCVD noted in CKD.
   - **When to use / contraindications:** Atorvastatin should be continued unless there is significant evidence of statin intolerance or myopathy. Monitoring liver function and muscle symptoms is advised [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19)].
   - **Supporting sources:** [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19)], [Ndumele et al Science and Clinical Management (ndumele_ckm_synopsis_2023_p19)].

3. **ACE Inhibitor/ARB (Add or adjust, e.g., lisinopril):**
   - **Why this patient:** Given the patient's hypertensive chronic kidney disease and fluid overload (E8770), optimizing hypertension management with an ACE inhibitor or ARB can further reduce proteinuria and cardiovascular events, enhancing renal protection [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17)].
   - **When to use / contraindications:** These agents are first-line in CKD with hypertension unless contraindicated by hyperkalemia or bilateral renal artery stenosis. Regular monitoring of renal function and serum potassium is critical [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17)].
   - **Supporting sources:** [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17)], [Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19)].

Fallback to option 2 (statin adjustment) if SGLT2 inhibitors are not feasible due to eGFR concerns below 20 mL/min/1.73 m² or volume status issues. Use option 3 if further optimization of antihypertensive therapy is needed and statin intolerance is present or SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated.

### References
- Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p19): SGLT2 inhibitor effectiveness in CKD and ASCVD.
- Ndumele et al AHA Presidential Advisory (ndumele_aha_ckm_2023_p17): Recommendations for ACE inhibitors/ARBs in CKD.
- Ndumele et al Science and Clinical Management (ndumele_ckm_synopsis_2023_p19): Statin use in high cardiovascular risk.
